mainText:
If you only remember one thing today, make it this: China just had its biggest A I week of the year. Xi Jinping showed up at the World A I Conference for the first time. Moonshot's K three model rattled markets. And an eight point five billion dollar memory chip I P O landed. The center of gravity in the A I race keeps shifting east.

Section one. China's A I week, from your Reads.
Your Recode China A I digest reports that Xi Jinping attended the World A I Conference for the first time, a clear signal of top level state backing. Moonshot A I's new K three model shook markets, echoing the DeepSeek moment. And a memory chip maker raised eight and a half billion dollars in an I P O. Why it matters: state backing, hot models, and big capital mean China's A I ecosystem is compounding fast, right as U S export policy wobbles.

Section two. A I models and releases.
Open A I's G P T five point six family went public in three tiers, after an unusual customer by customer review by the Commerce Department. Its new G P T Live can listen and speak at the same time, handling interruptions and live translation. Anthropic launched Claude Sonnet five, near flagship performance at a fraction of the price, and the U S lifted national security restrictions on its Fable five and Mythos five models. Meta announced Muse Spark one point one, an agentic model with a million token context that can use computers and delegate to parallel sub agents. Google DeepMind shipped fast new image and video generation models. And Open A I is shutting down its standalone Atlas browser to fold everything into one Chat G P T super app. Why it matters: frontier models are now cheap, agentic, and everywhere, and governments are inserting themselves into release decisions.

Section three. A I policy and regulation.
In Washington, the bipartisan Great American A I Act draft would require frontier model disclosures and third party audits, and would block state A I development laws for three years. States aren't waiting: one hundred nine state A I laws are already on the books this year, trending toward child safety and data center rules. In Europe, a new action plan pairs the A I Act with cybersecurity coordination for advanced models. And from your Tech Policy Press newsletter: the World Cup is over, but the surveillance systems built for it are here to stay. Why it matters: the fight over federal preemption will decide who actually regulates A I in America.

Section four. Chips and tech geopolitics.
The old bipartisan consensus on China chip controls has fractured into a three way standoff between Congress, the White House, and the chip industry. En vidia's H two hundred chips can now go to China case by case, with a fifty percent volume cap and a twenty five percent tariff. Analysts say the global chip world is splitting in two, with separate supply chains, architectures, and A I infrastructure. Why it matters: export controls have become improvisational economic statecraft, and companies are planning for a divided tech world.

Section five. Your media and comms corner.
From your Reads: The Information's Weekender looks at how Trump's A I agenda is colliding with reality on the ground, and why Andreessen Horowitz's newest investor left Apple. And the T B M newsletter offers a simple model for A I success inside organizations: what matters is the denominator, how many people actually adopt it. Why it matters for your work: corporate A I stories are shifting from capability hype to adoption and governance.

Section six. Tech for good.
Geneva's A I for Good Summit launched a new global commission with more than forty founding members, including heads of state and U N agencies. The World Food Programme is preparing remote steered A I aid trucks for field tests in Uganda. The U N refugee agency unveiled an A I legal assistant for lawyers representing refugees. And surgeons in China implanted a coin sized brain computer interface in a patient with a spinal cord injury. That's the top of the news. Your full briefing is in the recording.
